Phase-79.NAME: Cyber Title Polish and Promo-Safe MTProto Anti-Abuse
Phase-79.objective: Polish the public/auth cyber title typography and harden MTProto anti-abuse around the intended promotion-sharing model: observe, alert, and wait for explicit admin action.

scope.in-scope: Public/auth cyber title styling, acid-pink creative typography, responsive no-overlap checks, MTProto abuse severity policy, admin-only alert handling, promotion-sharing copy/contract, and regression tests for no automatic proxy/IP blocking.
scope.out-of-scope: Changing install/deploy scripts, nginx, docker-compose, MTProto runtime topology, VPN runtime, Telegram promotion tag format, user proxy link generation, automatic MTProto proxy disablement, or inspecting Telegram chats/channels/content.

requirements.requirement-1: The visible "Кибернетический Протокол Навигации" title on public/auth entry surfaces must use acid-pink color, stronger weight, and a more creative cyber typography treatment without external CDN dependencies.
requirements.requirement-2: The / public splash contract remains fast and compact; if the title is rendered on /, it must not delay or block the automatic redirect to /login.
requirements.requirement-3: MTProto individual proxies may be shared intentionally for KrotPN promotion so Telegram clients see the configured promo channel tag.
requirements.requirement-4: Sharing or many-IP observation alone must not be treated as hard abuse.
requirements.requirement-5: MTProto anti-abuse must continue to record signals and create admin alerts, but it must never automatically disable proxies or block IP addresses.
requirements.requirement-6: Only the administrator can disable an MTProto proxy, block an IP with TTL, or close an alert after explicit confirmation.

architecture.flow-1: M-073 identifies the auth/splash title surfaces and preserves the Phase-67 public redirect and auth state contracts.
architecture.flow-2: M-071 owns the reusable acid-pink cyber title class, creative font stack, neon treatment, mobile-safe sizing, and reduced-motion fallback.
architecture.flow-3: M-056 classifies MTProto telemetry in observe-only mode and treats promo-sharing traffic as allowed unless hard concurrent/traffic/error evidence is present.
architecture.flow-4: M-060 creates durable high/critical admin alerts while preserving explicit admin confirmation for every enforcement action.
architecture.flow-5: M-058 renders operator-facing alert context without exposing technical Signals, secrets, promo tag values, or Telegram links.
architecture.flow-6: M-059 remains the promotion-tag owner; Phase-79 must not rotate SNI, change user links, or expose the stored tag.

planned-interfaces.ui-1: CSS selector or route-local class for the cyber title, applied consistently to public/auth entry surfaces.
planned-interfaces.policy-1: Promo-safe MTProto abuse policy: shared proxy usage is allowed; high/critical alert thresholds require strong multi-IP concurrency, extreme traffic, or repeated error evidence.
planned-interfaces.policy-2: No new automatic enforcement switch is introduced for MTProto; existing disable/block routes remain admin-confirmed only.

steps.step-1: Map all "Кибернетический Протокол Навигации" render points and decide whether / splash needs visible title text or only the existing auth surfaces need title restyling.
steps.step-2: Add a compact acid-pink cyber title style in M-071 with stronger font weight, creative bundled/system font stack, neon treatment, and reduced-motion/readability safeguards.
steps.step-3: Apply the title style through M-073 without changing auth API calls, token/session behavior, registration pending state, or the / to /login redirect.
steps.step-4: Add frontend smoke coverage for the title color/style marker, responsive no-overlap, no external font/CDN dependency, and reduced-motion fallback.
steps.step-5: Review M-056 severity thresholds so normal proxy sharing, many IPs, and mobile/network churn stay observe-only medium unless paired with hard abuse evidence.
steps.step-6: Strengthen M-056 tests for promo-sharing allowed behavior, many-IP no-alert behavior, and high/critical alert handoff only under hard abuse conditions.
steps.step-7: Strengthen M-060 tests to prove alerts never disable proxy assignments or block IPs without explicit admin confirmation.
steps.step-8: Update M-058 admin UI copy or markers so the operator understands that sharing is expected and alerts represent hard abuse candidates only.
steps.step-9: Verify M-059 promotion tag state remains masked, does not rotate issued links, and remains independent of anti-abuse alerts.
steps.step-10: Run frontend build, frontend-admin build if UI marker changes touch admin code, backend MTProto analytics/admin-alert tests, Phase-79 smoke, XML parse, MyGRACE lint, git diff check, and protected deploy/install/runtime guard.

mental-walkthroughs.walkthrough-1: Normal promo sharing: one user shares a proxy link; many people connect from different IPs over time; analytics records usage/IP metadata, many-IP signals remain observe-only medium, and no proxy/IP is blocked.
mental-walkthroughs.walkthrough-2: Hard abuse candidate: one assignment shows many simultaneous connections from many IPs plus extreme traffic or repeated protocol errors; M-056 creates high/critical signal, M-060 creates an open admin alert, and access stays enabled until admin acts.
mental-walkthroughs.walkthrough-3: Admin enforcement: the operator reviews the alert, confirms disable proxy or TTL IP block, M-060 archives the alert with audit-safe action result, and M-059 promotion tag state remains unchanged.

risks.risk-1: Over-aggressive MTProto thresholds could punish the intended promotion-sharing model; mitigate by making sharing/many-IP alone medium observe-only.
risks.risk-2: Too-soft thresholds could hide real abuse; mitigate by alerting on combined hard evidence such as high concurrent multi-IP activity, traffic spikes, and repeated errors.
risks.risk-3: Creative title styling could harm readability on mobile; mitigate with responsive smoke and no-overlap markers at small viewports.
risks.risk-4: Promotion tag or proxy secrets could leak into UI/tests; mitigate with existing redaction guards and Phase-79 smoke checks.

acceptance.item-1: Cyber title uses acid-pink, heavier creative styling and remains readable on mobile/tablet/desktop.
acceptance.item-2: Frontend/auth behavior remains unchanged apart from approved visual title styling.
acceptance.item-3: MTProto promo-sharing usage is explicitly allowed and does not trigger automatic enforcement.
acceptance.item-4: High/critical MTProto abuse creates admin alerts only; disable/block actions require explicit admin confirmation.
acceptance.item-5: Verification passes without deploy/install/runtime topology changes.

Phase-79.evidence: PASS local: Phase-79 smoke passed; backend MTProto analytics/admin-alert/promotion-tag tests passed 8/8; frontend and frontend-admin builds passed; Phase-43/54/42 and Phase-56 regressions passed; XML parse, MyGRACE lint, git diff --check, and Phase-79 protected deploy/install/runtime guard passed. Phase-67/59/61 broad protected-diff smokes were not used as final gates because they intentionally fail on the already-open Phase-78/79 backend diff while their UI/build/bundle checks remain covered by targeted Phase-79, Phase-56, and build evidence.
